statusLine: European fuel stocks remain under pressure with 15 of 27 countries showing critical levels in at least one fuel type, while Brent crude has surged 45% since late February disruptions.
fullAnalysis:
EU fuel security remains under strain despite average stock levels appearing adequate. While EU-wide averages show petrol at 99.2 days, diesel at 97.6 days, and jet fuel at 91.4 days - all above the 90-day mandatory minimum - the distribution reveals significant vulnerabilities. Romania faces the most acute shortage with all three fuel types in critical status, including petrol reserves at just 33.2 days. Poland, Luxembourg, and Germany also show critical levels across multiple fuel categories.

The data reveals a clear pattern of eastern and central European countries experiencing the most severe shortages, likely reflecting their greater exposure to Middle East supply disruptions. Of particular concern is jet fuel availability, with 17 of 27 countries showing critical or warning levels, including major aviation hubs like Germany (61.4 days) and France (59.6 days). This could significantly impact air travel and cargo operations if conditions deteriorate further.

Oil prices underscore the supply pressure, with Brent crude reaching $105.26 per barrel - a 45.2% increase since late February's escalation began. This price surge is flowing through to consumers, with pump prices averaging €1.764 per liter for petrol and €1.943 for diesel. The Netherlands shows the highest petrol prices at €2.279 per liter, while Malta offers the cheapest at €1.34, highlighting significant regional price disparities across the bloc.
keyPoints: 15 of 27 EU countries have critical fuel stock levels in at least one category, with Romania showing the most severe shortages across all fuel types
Jet fuel supplies are particularly tight, with 17 countries below safe levels and major hubs like Germany and France showing critical status
Eastern European countries face the most acute shortages, with Poland, Romania, and Lithuania among the worst affected
Brent crude has surged 45.2% to $105.26 since late February disruptions began, driving consumer fuel prices higher across the EU
Despite regional vulnerabilities, EU-wide average stock levels remain above the 90-day mandatory minimum for all fuel types
